findings.claim: In the hot training path, `unshard_parameters()` (once per FSDP unit per forward/microbatch) and `reduce_gradients()` (once per FSDP unit per backward/microbatch) call `self._unsharded_model_weight.rendezvous(gather_axis)` / `partial_grad.rendezvous(reduce_axis)` on every invocation when use_symm_mem is enabled. `DBuffer.rendezvous()` calls `symm_mem.rendezvous(self.local_buffer, group=...)` which is a rendezvous/registration operation that involves a barrier/handshake across ranks. Unlike the inference path (megatron/core/inference/symmetric_memory.py) which rendezvouses ONCE at buffer allocation and caches `symm_mem_hdl`, here the rendezvous is re-issued on every unshard/reduce step. The `_unsharded_model_weight` buffer additionally has its storage released and reallocated (release_storage/reallocate_storage) each step, which may force re-rendezvous, but the rendezvous should still be memoized/handled once per allocation rather than per collective.

findings.claim: This is an intentional revert that RE-INTRODUCES a `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` (plus per-model-chunk `free_overlap_buffers()`) call on the checkpoint-save path in `save_checkpoint_and_time`. Mechanically this is the classic deferred-free-peak pattern: buffers ARE eventually reclaimed, but the eager `empty_cache()` releases blocks back to the driver so the caching allocator must re-`cudaMalloc` them on the next iteration, adding a synchronous device-wide stall at every checkpoint. However, in this specific case the call is a deliberate correctness/OOM workaround (the async-checkpoint worker process needs GPU headroom for D2H transfers), so the throughput cost at checkpoint boundaries is accepted on purpose rather than an accidental regression. `free_overlap_buffers()` also `wait()`s on any pending param-gather handle, serializing that comm against the save.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure per-iteration time on the checkpoint-save iterations (vs non-save iterations) before/after this change, with overlap param-gather enabled, to quantify the empty_cache + re-cudaMalloc stall amortized over the save interval.

findings.claim: In `get_grouped_rollouts`, the new consume-at-batch-granularity path introduces a tight retry loop: `if consume_at_batch_granularity: while not await generate_and_enqueue(batch_id, 0): pass`. When `filter_groups_with_same_reward` is active and a generated group is rejected (all-equal rewards -> np.std <= 1e-6), the coroutine immediately retries generating a *new* full group with no backpressure and without releasing/re-acquiring the submission gate. Previously (parent) a rejected group decremented `submitted_groups`, released the semaphore, and let the `while` loop re-acquire, naturally interleaving with the gate. The new inline `while ... pass` loop keeps the gate slot held and re-submits full inference generations back-to-back until one passes, which can generate substantially more inference work (wasted rollouts) and can pin a worker on repeated generation attempts. This is a throughput/latency regression on the filtered path.

findings.claim: Side effect of the (correct) budget split: because scratch_bytes = MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST * max_requests * per_slot_bytes is reserved FIRST, and max_requests auto-derives from buffer_size_gb, a large KV buffer yields large scratch reservation and therefore far fewer durable prefix-cache slots for the same prefix_caching_mamba_gb. Users keeping their previous (now under-sized) budget will get fewer cached block boundaries -> lower prefix-cache hit rate -> reduced prefill-skip latency/throughput benefit (the test files raise budgets 0.05->2.0 and 0.2->4.0 GB to compensate). This is a behavioral consequence of enforcing the correct budget, not a new allocation bug, so severity is low; worth flagging so budgets are re-tuned when max_requests is large.

Add `hidden_states.record_stream(SharedExpertMLP.stream)` (and record_stream on any intermediate side-stream tensors read back on the main stream).

Unlike the consensus path (which yields/awaits an all-reduce that naturally rate-limits and lets an idle engine settle), `sleep(0)` only yields to the event loop without any real backoff, so an idle rank burns a full GPU dummy_forward + CUDA sync per event-loop tick. This is exactly the busy-poll pattern flagged in the historical `spin-wait-hang` case (`while True: engine_step(); await asyncio.sleep(0)`). The added unit test even asserts `idle_calls > 0` within 0.2s, confirming intentional spinning. For ep_world_size==1 (the documented safe use case) there is no peer needing the collective, so these dummy_forwards on every idle tick are pure wasted GPU+CPU work.

findings.claim: The grouped-GEMM launch config is now derived purely from a heuristic (`_get_default_config`) keyed on M=num_tokens_hint, E, top_k, replacing the previous `@triton.autotune` over ~25 configs keyed on (N,K) plus the `_select_block_size_m` split. This is exactly the historical failure mode: hand-picking BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages for one shape/device profile and shipping it as a single default. The heuristic thresholds (block_n=64 iff M<=64, block_k=128 iff M<=64, num_warps=8 iff M>128, num_stages=4 iff M<=32, GROUP_SIZE_M=16 iff M//E>128) are static and not device-aware. On GPUs with smaller shared memory (e.g. L4-class, as in PR #11589) the large-M branch (BLOCK_SIZE_M=128, N=128, K=64, num_warps=8) plus the FUSE_SQUARED_RELU fp32 accumulator can exceed SMEM and hurt occupancy; on shapes where the removed autotuner would have picked N=256 or K=32 configs the fixed heuristic can be measurably slower. Losing autotune over (N,K) means no per-(E,N,K,device) adaptation.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Sweep vllm_fused_moe FC1/FC2 latency across (E, N, K, num_tokens_hint) at decode (M small) and prefill (M large) on multiple GPUs (H100 and a smaller-SMEM part), comparing the removed autotuned path vs the new _get_default_config heuristic.

findings.claim: The grouped-GEMM grid is changed from a persistent grid (grid = num_sms, CTAs stride over device-computed total_tiles) to a host-sized grid derived from num_tokens_hint: grid_size = num_pid_m_hint * num_pid_n, where num_pid_m_hint comes from em_hint = effective_tokens*topk + BLOCK_SIZE_M*num_local_experts. When the actual num_tokens_post_padded exceeds the hint (prefill spikes, or any step where per-step token count > num_tokens_hint), each CTA strides over multiple tiles via `tl.range(pid_init, total_tiles, grid_size)`, serializing work and reducing parallelism — the code comment explicitly acknowledges 'reduced parallelism on rare prefill spikes'. If num_tokens_hint is under-set relative to real traffic, this becomes a systematic latency regression rather than a rare one. This is a parallel-decomposition change (occupancy redesign), not just a knob tune.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Benchmark grouped-GEMM latency when actual per-step tokens exceed num_tokens_hint by 2-8x (mixed prefill/decode batches) vs the old persistent num_sms grid.

findings.claim: In `_get_decode_A_neg_exp`, during CUDA-graph capture (`torch.cuda.is_current_stream_capturing()` True) the code executes `self._A_neg_exp_cache.copy_(-torch.exp(self.A_log.float()))` unconditionally. This records the fp32-cast/exp/neg elementwise kernels INTO the captured graph. On every subsequent `.replay()` the copy re-runs, so the promised savings of 'three small elementwise kernels per token' are NOT eliminated on the CUDA-graph decode path — they are baked into the graph and replayed each step, negating the intended optimization on the exact hot path CUDA graphs target. The stale-flag fast path (`self._A_neg_exp_cache_stale`) only benefits the eager (non-captured) decode path.

findings.claim: CASE 2 of _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd was rewritten from a single fused RoPE application over the whole packed tensor into a per-sequence Python loop. Parent code built freqs_packed = torch.cat([...]) once and made ONE call to _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd on the entire packed batch. The new code loops `for x in sequence_splits`, calling _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd separately per packed sequence, and then does an extra `output.narrow(0, offset, ...).copy_(output_slice)` per sequence. Each _apply_rotary_pos_emb_bshd invocation launches several elementwise kernels (torch.cos, torch.sin, _rotate_half's chunk/cat, the multiply-add, and final torch.cat). This de-fuses what was one batched set of kernel launches into N-batches (one per packed sequence) plus N copy kernels, multiplying kernel-launch and intermediate-allocation overhead by the number of packed sequences on this attention hot path (called for both query and key in every layer). This is the RoPE unfused-elementwise / de-fusion pattern from the retrieved history.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Microbenchmark _apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd (CASE 2, freqs=max-seqlen) with cu_seqlens containing many short packed sequences (e.g. 32-128 sequences), cp_size=1 and cp_size>1, comparing parent single-cat-single-apply vs new per-sequence loop for per-call latency and kernel count.

findings.claim: The new CASE 2 path adds `output = torch.empty_like(t)` plus a per-sequence `output.narrow(0, output_offset, x.size(0)).copy_(output_slice)`. This introduces an extra full-size output buffer allocation and N explicit copy kernels that did not exist in the parent (which returned the result of a single batched apply directly). These copies are pure layout/scatter overhead relative to the fused version.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Profile the per-sequence output.narrow().copy_() copies vs returning a single concatenated apply result under many packed sequences.

findings.claim: `reshard_model_weights` now unconditionally calls `_harmonize_buffer_dtypes`, which issues an `all_gather_object` over `group` on EVERY refit/swap invocation. Refit is a repeated hot path (the module explicitly caches the plan and CopyService precisely because `swap_model_weights` is called many times, e.g. once per RL weight-sync/refit iteration). Unlike the plan build (guarded by `_plan_cache`) and service creation (guarded by `_service_cache`), this new collective has no caching/guard, so it runs a full pickled object-based all-gather every time even though buffer dtypes only need harmonizing once (they don't change across refit steps after the first fp32 upcast). `all_gather_object` is especially expensive: it pickles the dict, does a size all-gather plus a byte all-gather, forcing a CPU<->GPU/host round-trip and synchronization on every call. This is well-formed on all ranks (every rank participates, so it is not a hang), but it adds a per-iteration serialized collective barrier to the refit critical path.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time repeated swap_model_weights() calls (e.g. 100 iterations) on a MoE model with a modest world size and compare wall time / collective count with and without _harmonize_buffer_dtypes; confirm the all_gather_object fires every iteration.

findings.claim: In the non-`index==-1` branch, `read_tb_logs_as_list` previously loaded and `Reload()`ed exactly one event file (`files[index]`). The refactor now calls `_load_event_accumulators_with_scalars(files)`, which constructs an `EventAccumulator` and calls `.Reload()` on EVERY event file in the directory (fully parsing each with `SIZE_GUIDANCE=0`, i.e. loading all scalars/tensors into memory) before discarding all but the single selected accumulator. For the common case `index=0`, this changes O(1) file-load work into O(N) file-load work where N is the number of tfevents files present. This is redundant-load-startup-work: files that are loaded/parsed and then thrown away.
findings.suggested_benchmark: Time read_tb_logs_as_list(path, index=0) on a directory containing many (e.g. 20+) tfevents files, comparing parent vs this commit.

findings.claim: The parent build step used a rich cache-from list: PR-specific buildcache, main-buildcache, and the 100 most-recently-merged PRs' buildcaches (steps.cache_from.outputs.LAST_PRS). The diff collapses cache-from to a SINGLE donor: `type=registry,ref=.../megatron-lm:0-buildcache` (SEED is hardcoded to "0"). Removing the PR-specific and main donors narrows the warm-cache surface. For a PR whose Dockerfile.ci from-source layers already have a warm ${PR_NUMBER}-buildcache (its own previous run) or a fresh main-buildcache, this change means BuildKit can no longer seed from that closer cache and must import only the 0-buildcache baseline. If the 0-baseline is stale relative to the PR's tree, downstream layers miss and the build performs a longer cold rebuild. This is a plausible build-time regression on the hot path (every PR push runs this build).
findings.suggested_benchmark: Measure end-to-end docker/build-push-action wall time on (a) a repeat push to an existing PR (previously would hit its own ${PR}-buildcache) and (b) a PR branched off a recently-updated main, comparing parent (multi-donor cache-from) vs this commit (single 0-buildcache donor). Track cache-hit ratio per layer via BuildKit --progress=plain.
